review_summary: This unit supports conceptual questions about BLEU, brevity penalties, and the limitations of reference-overlap evaluation for translation.

question_bank.question: What is the core idea behind BLEU as an automatic machine-translation metric?
choices: Measure n-gram overlap between a system translation and one or more human reference translations.
Ask a human evaluator to assign a fluency score to every sentence.
Check whether the translation uses exactly the same number of words as the source.
Train a language model to predict the next word in the target sentence.
question_bank.explanation: BLEU compares a candidate translation with human references by counting matching word sequences. This gives an automatic approximation of translation quality without requiring human scoring for every experiment.

question_bank.question: Why does BLEU include a brevity penalty?
choices: To discourage systems from translating only short, easy fragments while ignoring difficult parts.
To reward translations that are always shorter than the source sentence.
To force all candidates to have exactly the same length as the reference.
To penalize any translation that uses paraphrases.
question_bank.explanation: Without a brevity penalty, a system could get high precision on a few translated words while omitting much of the sentence. Penalizing overly short outputs helps push systems toward complete translations.

question_bank.question: Which limitation follows from BLEU's dependence on reference n-gram overlap?
choices: A good paraphrastic translation can score poorly if its wording differs from the references.
BLEU cannot compare a candidate translation to more than one reference.
BLEU always gives 100 to any fluent translation.
BLEU only works for classification tasks with a single correct label.
question_bank.explanation: Translation often has many acceptable phrasings. A reference-overlap metric can miss valid paraphrases because it mainly rewards matching local word sequences.
